coredata.dc:title: Simple and user-friendly nucleolus imaging with ultra-narrow bandwidth red emission carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kalji, Seyed-Omid
Sadeghizadeh, Majid
Khajeh, Khosro
coredata.dc:description: The nucleolus has emerged as a promising target for cancer diagnosis and therapy, with its imaging being crucial for both fundamental research and clinical applications. Fluorescence imaging is a powerful tool for this purpose. However, its use is limited by the scarcity of fluorescent agents that specifically target the nucleolus and have excellent biocompatibility. Carbon dots (CDs) are becoming a valuable tool for cellular imaging because of their excellent optical properties and low toxicity. In this work, for the first time, ultra-narrow bandwidth emission CDs were synthesized with nicotinamide (NAM) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA) for nucleolus imaging. The reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 8 h in a stainless-steel autoclave, using a facile, low-cost, and one-step solvothermal approach with H2O/H2SO4 as solvent. Prepared CDs emit red emission at 617 nm with an ultra-narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 28.1 nm and quantum yield (QY) of 13 %. Moreover, the cytotoxicity assay demonstrated that CDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility with MCF-7 breast cancer cells, even at concentrations as high as 4.5 mg/mL. Finally, fixed and live cell imaging results indicate that prepared CDs show bright red fluorescence in the nucleoli of MCF-7 cells. Our results demonstrate the efficacy of prepared CDs in nucleolus imaging, offering a convenient and promising fluorescent probe that can be used for fixed and live cell nucleolus imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Imaging
Narrow FWHM
Nucleolus

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of concentration, time, and solvent on the CDs
Interactions of CDs with RNA, DNA, and other substances

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support provided by Tarbiat Modares University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis spectra for CDs, NAM, and o-PDA, (b) Emission spectra of CDs under excitation from wavelengths 360 to 580. Inset image: CDs under daylight and UV light (365 nm), (c) Optimization of CDs fluorescence intensity based on the concentration (λex = 560 nm), (d) FWHM and Stokes shift of CDs (λex = 560 nm), (e) Stability test of CDs during 180 days (λex = 560 nm), and (f) Stability test of CDs in different solvents (λex = 560 nm).

(a) HRTEM images of CDs in 50 nm and 10 nm scale bar with size distribution histogram (inset images), (b) FT-IR spectra of NAM, o-PDA, and CDs, (c) XRD pattern of CDs, (d) Raman spectrum of CDs, and (e) Cell viability of CDs in different concentrations using MTT assay (24 h).
Inverted fluorescence microscope and confocal laser scanning microscope images of fixed (a) and live (b) MCF-7 cells in the presence of CDs.

(a) Inverted fluorescence microscope images of non-treated and treated MCF-7 cells with DNase I or RNase A in the presence of CDs, (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the absence and presence of RNA or DNA (λex = 560 nm), and (c) Fluorescence intensity of CDs upon the addition of different substances (λex = 560 nm).
The steps of synthesis and purification of CDs.
The steps of cell imaging in the live (a) and fixed (b) states.

head.ce:title: Simple and user-friendly nucleolus imaging with ultra-narrow bandwidth red emission carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Genetics, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Genetics, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nucleolus has emerged as a promising target for cancer diagnosis and therapy, with its imaging being crucial for both fundamental research and clinical applications. Fluorescence imaging is a powerful tool for this purpose. However, its use is limited by the scarcity of fluorescent agents that specifically target the nucleolus and have excellent biocompatibility. Carbon dots (CDs) are becoming a valuable tool for cellular imaging because of their excellent optical properties and low toxicity. In this work, for the first time, ultra-narrow bandwidth emission CDs were synthesized with nicotinamide (NAM) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA) for nucleolus imaging. The reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 8 h in a stainless-steel autoclave, using a facile, low-cost, and one-step solvothermal approach with H2O/H2SO4 as solvent. Prepared CDs emit red emission at 617 nm with an ultra-narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 28.1 nm and quantum yield (QY) of 13 %. Moreover, the cytotoxicity assay demonstrated that CDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility with MCF-7 breast cancer cells, even at concentrations as high as 4.5 mg/mL. Finally, fixed and live cell imaging results indicate that prepared CDs show bright red fluorescence in the nucleoli of MCF-7 cells. Our results demonstrate the efficacy of prepared CDs in nucleolus imaging, offering a convenient and promising fluorescent probe that can be used for fixed and live cell nucleolus imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The nucleolus, typically found in the nucleus and abundant in RNA (particularly rRNA), is the most vital subnuclear structure in cells. Alterations in the number or shape of nucleoli can be linked to various diseases, notably cancer, making the nucleolus an ideal target for early cancer diagnosis and treatment. Thus, imaging the nucleolus is highly important for both fundamental research and clinical applications [1]. Various materials, such as gold nanoclusters [2], metal chelates [3], organic luminescent molecules [4], and CdS quantum dots [5] have been successfully used for nucleolus imaging. However, these materials have certain drawbacks, including the need for specific targeting group modifications, complex preparation procedures, and potential cytotoxicity, which limit their practical applications in nucleolus imaging. Currently, SYTO RNASelect is the only widely accepted commercial nucleolus imaging probe. However, its high cost, strict storage requirements, and limitation to staining and imaging only fixed cells restrict its use in live cell applications [6]. Consequently, there is a significant need to develop new fluorescent probes for nucleolus imaging.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a group of carbon nanomaterials that are zero-dimensional and usually have a size of less than 10 nm [7–9]. They have exceptional qualities that are advantageous for cell imaging such as adjustable photoluminescence and easy synthesis process. In addition, compared to many traditional dyes, CDs offer benefits such as being cost-effective, having high optical stability with minimal photoblinking or photobleaching, and exhibiting lower cell toxicity [10]. In addition, red-emitting CDs have more advantages because of deep tissue penetration, negligible autofluorescence, and low tissue absorption [11–13]. Moreover, narrow bandwidth emissive CDs can have an advantage over common CDs because they display great color purity and can be used for parallel imaging [14]. In summary, the development of red-emitting CDs with an ultra-narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) is important and meaningful in cell imaging because the broad, excitation-dependent, and short-wavelength emission of CDs creates significant challenges for co-staining different subcellular structures and live imaging.

A recent focus in research is discovering new precursors that can produce narrow bandwidth emissive CDs. On the other hand, CDs can target the nucleolus due to intrinsic functional groups retained from their precursors, making the choice of raw materials crucial for targeting [6]. RNA-selective dyes typically feature aromatic rings, nitrogen-containing heterocycles, and amines [15]. Thus, selecting appropriate carbon and nitrogen sources is effective for the synthesis of narrow bandwidth emissive CDs for nucleolus imaging. Recently, several CDs have been developed for nucleolus imaging most of them have yellow or green emission [6,15–22]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are no existing reports on synthesizing ultra-narrow bandwidth red emission CDs (FWHM = 28.1 nm) using a simple method with nicotinamide (NAM) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA), without modifications, for fixed and live nucleolus imaging applications. The produced CDs have a relatively good quantum yield (QY), excellent photostability, superior biocompatibility, and a general inherent nucleolus imaging capability. The outstanding optical properties of the synthesized CDs make them highly promising for biomedical imaging applications.

NAM, o-PDA, sulfuric acid (H2SO4), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) components, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), rhodamine 6G, formaldehyde 37 % (FA), d-glucose (Glu), sucrose (Suc), hemoglobin (HGb), human serum albumin (HSA), ribonuclease A (RNase A), deoxyribonuclease I (DNase I), and glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Merck. Ethanol (EtOH), methanol (MeOH), isopropanol (IPA), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone (Ace), and glycerol (GC) were purchased from Dr. Mojallali of Tehran, Iran. Dialysis membrane (cut-off 3500 Da), trypsin-EDTA, high glucose Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), penicillin-streptomycin, and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific. The MCF-7 cell line was attained from the Pasteur Institute of Tehran, Iran.

The reaction solvent was prepared by dissolving 0.5 mL of H2SO4 in 29.5 mL of deionized water. Then, 32 mg of o-PDA and 36 mg of NAM were dissolved in the reaction solvent. The prepared mixture was poured into a 50 mL stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h in an oven. After cooling, the solution was centrifuged at 13,200 RCF for 10 min. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove impurities. Finally, the obtained solution was dialyzed against deionized water using a membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da. The steps of CD synthesis are shown in Scheme 1 .

The PerkinElmer LS-45 fluorescence and Cary 100 UV–Vis spectrophotometers were used to record the photoluminescent and UV–Vis absorption spectra. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), Raman, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis were performed using Nicolet IR 100, Thermo Nicolet Almega, and Philips X'Pert MPD (40 kV, λ = 1.54056 Å), respectively. A cytotoxicity assay was conducted using a BioTek ELx800 ELISA plate reader. Cell images were acquired on an inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus, CKX41) and confocal laser scanning (Zeiss LSM 800) microscope, while the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were captured by a FEI TECNAI G2 F20 at 200 KV.

To calculate the relative QY of CDs using rhodamine 6G as a reference, a solution of rhodamine 6G in absolute EtOH, which has a known QY of 95 %, was prepared. Additionally, a solution of the synthesized CDs in water was prepared. The absorbance of the rhodamine 6G solution at 530 nm, its highest absorption wavelength, was measured, along with the absorbance of the CD solution at the same wavelength. It was ensured that the absorbance of both solutions was below 0.1 to minimize reabsorption effects. Next, both the rhodamine 6G and CD solutions were excited with 530 nm light, and the integrated emission intensity of rhodamine 6G in absolute EtOH and the CDs in water was measured. The following formula was used to calculate the QY of the CDs:
(1)
Q Y
X
=
Q Y
S
×
A S
A X
I X
I S
(
η X
η S
)
2
In this formula, (QY
X ) denotes the QY of the CD sample, (QY
S ) denotes the QY of the standard substance (rhodamine 6G, 95 %), (A
X ), and (A
S ) refers to the absorbance values of the CD and standard sample at the excitation wavelength (530 nm), respectively. In addition, (I
X ) and (I
S ) refer to the integrated emission intensities of the CD and standard sample, respectively. Moreover, (η
X ) and (η
S ) represent the refractive indices of the solvents used for the standard (absolute EtOH, 1.36) and the CD sample (water, 1.33), respectively [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentration, time, and solvent on the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve optimal results in various fluorescence tests, the effect of different concentrations of CDs, with a maximum concentration of 32 mg/mL, on fluorescence intensity under 560 nm excitation was evaluated and optimized. Additionally, the fluorescence of CDs was measured at different times and various solvents (water, PBS, EtOH, MeOH, IPA, DMF, THF, Ace, GC, and FA) under 560 nm excitation using a spectrophotometer.

The MCF-7 cells were incubated for 24 h at a cell culture incubator with standard conditions (37 °C with 5 % CO2) using DMEM medium supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin (1:1) and then seeded in wells of a 96-well plate (8000 MCF-7 cells in each well). After 24 h, the medium was replaced with fresh medium containing increasing concentrations of CDs from 0 to 4.5 mg/mL and incubated again for 24 h. Next, the culture medium was removed and replaced with 100 μL of growth medium containing 10 μL of MTT solution (5.0 mg/mL in PBS) with another 4 h incubation. Finally, the culture medium was removed slowly, and 100 μL of DMSO was added to each well. After 15 min of shaking, the absorbance was recorded at 490 nm. All tests were repeated three times.

The MCF-7 cells were first incubated overnight in standard growth conditions and then seeded into a 24-well plate for 24 h. After removing the existing culture medium, a fresh medium containing synthesized CDs with a concentration of 200 μg/mL was added to each well. The cells were then incubated for times ranging from 0.1 to 30 min to allow CDs to enter the cells. It is important to note that the removal of CDs from the wells after each incubation time, washing with PBS, replacement with new fresh CD-free media, and subsequent fluorescent imaging were performed as quickly as possible to obtain desirable comparative results.

In order to live cell imaging, the MCF-7 cells were detached from the T25 cell culture flask and transferred into 35 mm cell culture petri dishes to incubate for 48 h in standard growth conditions. Then, the medium was removed and a new medium containing 200 μg/mL of prepared CDs was added to the petri dishes and incubated for 15 min. Finally, this medium was also removed, cells were washed three times with PBS, and a fresh medium (without CDs) was added to the cells before imaging (Scheme 2 a).

For fixed cell imaging, the MCF-7 cells were cultured in 35 mm cell culture petri dishes for 48 h and after removing the medium, cells were washed with PBS three times and incubated with 4 % formaldehyde solution for 10 min at room temperature. Cells were washed three times with PBS again and then incubated in CDs solution (200 μg/mL of CDs in PBS as solvent) for 15 min at room temperature. The cells were washed with PBS three times to remove the extra CDs and incubated with DAPI solution (2 μg/mL DAPI in PBS as solvent) at room temperature for 10 min under a dark environment. After that, the cells were washed three times and finally loaded with PBS before imaging (Scheme 2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interactions of CDs with RNA, DNA, and other substances
ce:section.ce:para: MCF-7 cells were seeded in an 8-well plate and treated with a culture medium containing either 25 μg/mL DNase I solution or 25 μg/mL RNase A solution at 37 °C for 2 h. The cells were then fixed with methanol and finally were incubated with 200 μg/mL CDs for 15 min before imaging. Notably, three washings with PBS were performed in each step. In addition, to examine the effect of RNA, DNA, and other substances on the fluorescence of CDs, the emission spectra of CDs were recorded under 560 nm excitation before and after incubation with RNA (200 μg/mL), DNA, (200 μg/mL), GSH, HGb, HSA, Glu, and Suc (0.1 mM) for 15 min using a spectrophotometer.

CDs were synthesized through the one-step solvothermal method by NAM and o-PDA as precursors. Fig. 1 a presents the UV–vis absorption spectra for both the precursors and the synthesized CDs. It is evident from the spectrum of NAM that there are two peaks at approximately 260 and 220 nm. The former is associated with the pyridine ring's π-π∗ transition, while the latter is associated with the carbonyl's n-π∗ transition on the amide group [24]. In addition, the π-π∗ transition is responsible for the bands in the o-PDA spectrum at roughly 220 and 230 nm, whereas the amino group is responsible for a strong band that occurs at 290 nm [25]. The spectrum of CDs showed that the absorption peak at around 220 nm was ascribed to the π–π∗ transition of the aromatic CC bond, while the n–π∗ transition of the sp2 system including CN or CO bonds was responsible for the absorption at 260 nm [26,27]. A few absorption peaks are also visible at 400–700 nm, which are associated with the other functionalized surface groups of CDs. The absorption in the visible region is helpful for the long-wavelength emission of CDs [28].

About the photoluminescence features, when the excitation wavelength was adjusted from 360 to 580 nm, the emission wavelength consistently stayed at 617 nm (Fig. 1b). This shows that the emission wavelength does not depend on the excitation wavelength. Additionally, the highest emission intensity occurred when excited at a wavelength of 560 nm. The nanoparticle solution when exposed to UV light (365 nm) emits red emission (Fig. 1b inset). Moreover, it was found that the synthesized CDs' QY was 13 % compared to rhodamine 6G. It is worth noting that the photoluminescence of synthesized CDs, similar to other fluorescent dyes, is influenced by concentration, and in highly concentrated solutions, self-quenching can occur [29]. Therefore, the concentration used in fluorescence tests was optimized and the highest emission intensity was observed at a concentration of 8 mg/mL (Fig. 1c). Consequently, this concentration has been used in all fluorescence tests. As shown in Fig. 1d, two other important features of the synthesized CDs are their ultra-narrow FWHM (28.1 nm) and their large Stokes shift (≈60 nm). Red, narrow, and bright emission of CDs can be attributed to carefully selecting precursors and reaction conditions [30]. According to the quantum confinement effect, when the size of the sp2-conjugated domain is smaller than the exciton Bohr radius, the quasi-continuous electron energy levels transform into two discrete levels (HOMO and LUMO). Therefore, the excitation of electrons leads them to travel from HOMO to LUMO and consequently losing of their received energy via fluorescent emission during the electron relaxation. Additionally, the greater variety of functional groups in the CD structure creates new electron relaxation pathways (surface states), resulting in emissions with various energies and colors. This makes the final emission of CDs broad in bandwidth and low in color purity [31–33]. Moreover, the intrinsic properties of the functional groups around the conjugated structure significantly influence the emission mechanism of CDs. For instance, using precursors with high nitrogen content can produce CDs with red emission and narrower bandwidth [28]. Consequently, employing NAM and o-PDA as nitrogen-rich precursors results in the formation of more uniform functional groups within the synthesized CDs. This nitrogen enrichment and uniformity in functional groups ultimately contribute to a narrower emission bandwidth.

Furthermore, the CDs exhibited remarkable stability in time and different solvents, making them ideal for imaging applications. To evaluate the optical stability of the synthesized CDs over time, their fluorescence intensity was monitored over a span of 180 days. Fig. 1e shows that the emission intensity of the sample measured immediately after synthesis did not significantly differ from the emission intensity measured after 180 days. In addition, the stability of CD fluorescence emission in different solvents was also investigated (Fig. 1f), and the results showed that its emission was not significantly affected in the presence of different solvents. These characteristics candidate the synthesized CDs perfect for imaging.

Fig. 2 a displays the CD's HRTEM images at a scale of 50 nm and 10 nm. The images show dispersed particles with a quasi-spherical shape and an average size of about 4 nm. In addition, the HRTEM image of CDs reveals clearly defined lattice fringes with an in-plane lattice spacing of 0.27 nm, which aligns with the (020) diffraction facets of graphitic carbon [34,35].

Fig. 2b displays the FT-IR spectra of NAM, o-PDA, and CDs. For NAM, two bands at 3366 and 3157 cm−1 are observed, corresponding to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the NH2 group, respectively. The CO bond of the amide results in a strong CO stretching band, with a slight frequency shift due to conjugation between the carbonyl group and the amide nitrogen. This mode is represented by a strong band at 1679 cm−1. A band at 1618 cm−1 is associated with the NH2 scissoring mode. The frequency at 1394 cm−1 is attributed to the C–N stretching vibrations. The in-plane NH2 rocking mode, which absorbs weakly to moderately, is found around 1125 cm−1 that a band at 1157 cm−1 is assigned to this mode. The pyridine ring stretching vibrations of NAM are observed at 1484 cm−1. The NH2 out-of-plane (NH2 wag) deformation frequency is located in the 600-750 cm−1 region that a band at 700 cm−1 assigned to the NH2 wagging mode. The CO in-plane deformation occurs around 610 cm−1, with a band at 602 cm−1 tentatively assigned to this mode [36–38]. In the FT-IR spectrum of o-PDA, the bands at 3386, 3364, 3289, and 3188 cm−1 are assigned to the N–H stretching vibrations of the NH2 group. The band observed in the 2800–3100 cm−1 region corresponds to the C–H bonds of the aromatic ring vibrations. The CC stretching mode creates the bands at 1590 and 1620 cm−1. The band at 1496 cm−1 is related to the C–N–H group mixing with C–C stretching and C–H bending. The interaction between N–H bending, C–H bending, and C–N stretching vibrations causes a band at 1330 cm−1. The band observed between 1112 and 1214 cm−1 is ascribed to the C–H bending vibrations, while the band at 1247 cm−1 is associated with C–N stretching [25,39]. In the spectrum of CDs, N–H and C–H stretching vibrations are responsible for the band observed around 3400 and 3000 cm−1, respectively [27,40,41]. CO stretching vibration is related to the peak at 1710 cm−1. The stretching vibrations of CC are responsible for the peak at 1623 cm−1 [42–44]. Furthermore, the C–N groups may be recognized by the peak at 1415 cm−1 [41]. The C–O bonds are connected to the peak, which is centered at 1120 cm−1 [45]. The absorption at 613 cm−1 was caused by angular deformations outside of the C–H aromatic rings [43]. As a result, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs differs from that of the precursors, indicating changes in the precursors during the solvothermal synthesis and their conversion into CDs.

The narrow and sharp peaks of XRD patterns in Fig. 2c show the crystallinity of CDs. This pattern with numerous peaks suggests that the material is polycrystalline. The presence of small peaks strongly suggests that there are many minor changes that potentially lead to similar but distinct structures [46,47]. Using Raman spectroscopy, the graphitic properties of the CDs were investigated; the resulting spectrum is displayed in Fig. 2d. The spectra show two Raman bands that correspond to the disordered band (D) and the crystalline band (G) at 1393 and 1571 cm−1, respectively. These bands are related to the sp3 and sp2 carbon moieties in the CDs. The G band denotes the existence of crystalline carbons with an ordered structure resembling graphite, while the D band is typically associated with amorphous carbons that are disordered in nature. One significant metric that is typically used to evaluate the degree of graphitization of carbon materials is ID/IG. Based on the calculation, the ID/IG value of the CDs was 0.73, suggesting that the material has a high degree of graphitic order and low surface defects, which is also consistent with the results of the HRTEM and XRD [48,49].

When considering CDs as a potential staining agent, their biocompatibility must be evaluated. To assess the cytotoxic effects of the CDs, an MTT assay was conducted. MCF-7 cells were exposed to different concentrations of CDs, ranging from 0 to 4.5 mg/mL, for a duration of 24 h. Generally, after 24 h of incubation with these nanoparticles at concentrations ranging from 0.03 to 0.5 mg/mL, minimal adverse effects on cell viability were observed, maintaining a viability rate above 95 % (Fig. 2e). It should be noted that with the increase in the concentration of nanoparticles, the cell viability decreases further, however, they still show good biocompatibility with ∼70–90 % cell viability. These results show the low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of these nanoparticles and also provide the possibility of using them as fluorescence probes for live cell imaging.

To investigate the cellular internalization process, the live cells were utilized to monitor the uptake of CDs by MCF-7 cells. Fig. 3 presents a series of cellular internalization images. Before the experiment, the existing medium is substituted with a new medium solution containing CDs. The synthesized CDs exhibit significant speed for entering the cells. Generally, the entry of CDs begins with the accumulation of nanoparticles around the cell membrane (0.1–0.5 min). Subsequently, they pass through the membrane via passive transportation [50], and enter the cytoplasm (1–1.5 min). Within 2–4 min, the nanoparticles accumulate in the cytoplasm but have not yet entered the nucleus. Once enough CDs have entered the cytoplasm, these nanoparticles enter the nucleus and gradually begin to localize in the nucleolus region (7–10 min). At this stage, the nuclei show a faint color, but the nucleoli are distinguishable. In the final stage (15 min), the nucleoli of the cells are clearly and brightly stained and are fully recognizable. Additionally, the cytoplasm of the cells is filled with nanoparticles and glows, but not as brightly as the nucleoli. No significant change in the distribution and intensity of cell staining is observed within 15–30 min, indicating that the entire staining process is completed continuously and rapidly within 15 min.

Finally, the CDs were used in both fixed and live cell imaging assays. Inverted fluorescence microscope and confocal laser scanning microscope images utilizing fixed and live MCF-7 breast cancer cells are shown in Fig. 4 . For samples, a cell nucleolus staining pattern was seen. To verify the positioning of CDs within cells, the nuclei were stained with DAPI in the fixed cell imaging (Fig. 4a). It was found that CDs predominantly reside in the nucleoli. It turned out that CDs could stain nucleoli in a selective manner. Inside the nucleus are highly active areas called nucleoli, which are crucial for maintaining cellular homeostasis and they contain many enzyme complexes [51]. These results are better defined in live cell imaging (Fig. 4b). After penetrating cells, the CDs emit red emission when exposed to light. CDs can pass through the cell membrane and stain the cytoplasm. Also, after that, it can enter the nucleus and accumulate in the nucleolar area.

Previous reports discovered that the CDs made from m-PDA/p-PDA exhibited a strong affinity for the nucleoli of HeLa and A549 cancer cells. Following a thorough investigation and comparison with SYTO RNASelect, they postulated that the primary mechanism by which CDs stain nucleolus may be the selective interaction between RNA and CDs [17,52]. Given that our CDs can stain nucleolus and to a lesser extent cytoplasm, we hypothesize that prepared CDs may attach to RNA. Given that most RNAs in the nucleus, particularly rRNA, accumulate in the nucleoli, the mechanism by which CDs target the nucleolus was further investigated. Initially, the impact of DNA/RNA digestion on the cellular localization of CDs was examined. MCF-7 cells were treated with DNase I (which breaks down DNA) and then fixed; the CDs continued to stain the nucleolus more prominently than other nuclear regions (Fig. 5 a), suggesting that DNA digestion has minimal effect. Conversely, when cells were treated with RNase A (which breaks down RNA) and then fixed, the nucleolar region was not clearly stained by CDs, likely due to the reduced RNA content in the nucleoli. These results are consistent with other reports that used different types of CDs for imaging of nucleolus, nucleus, and nucleic acid [17,53–55]. In addition, the interaction between CDs, RNA, and other substances, including DNA and protein, was analyzed using fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 5b, adding RNA to the CD solution resulted in fluorescence enhancement, whereas adding DNA, protein, and other substances caused a decrease in fluorescence (Fig. 5c). Different fluorescence responses of CDs to RNA and DNA may be attributed to the structural differences between the two nucleic acids, affecting their interaction with CDs [17].

In conclusion, CDs can effectively achieve selective nucleolus targeted imaging in both fixed and live cells. These intriguing findings clearly showed that CDs' fluorescence characteristics are attainable in living cells, highlighting the potential benefit of CDs as intelligent luminescent labels in a complicated biological environment. The number of commercially accessible compounds that can specifically stain nucleoli is low. The photoluminescence displayed by CDs and their staining pattern allows them to be used in cell imaging research in the future and offers a new tool for nucleoli studies using fluorescence microscopy that is connected to health and disease states.

In summary, we fabricated nucleolus targeting red emissive CDs using NAM and o-PDA through a one-step solvothermal (H2O/H2SO4 as solvent) approach. The synthesized CDs exhibited an optimal emission wavelength of 617 nm, an ultra-narrow FWHM of 28.1 nm, a Stokes shift of 60 nm, excitation-independent fluorescence emission, high photostability, and a relatively high QY (13 %). In addition to their exceptional optical characteristics, the synthesized CDs demonstrated excellent biocompatibility (up to 4.5 mg/mL) based on MTT assay results. Furthermore, the CDs effectively imaged nucleoli in MCF-7 cells in both fixed and live states without any modifications, confirming their potential as a sustainable fluorescent probe. This work opens up a new, simple, and cost-effective route for synthesizing ultra-narrow red emission CDs for future practical applications in biomedicine.

Seyed-Omid Kalji: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Majid Sadeghizadeh: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Khosro Khajeh: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support provided by Tarbiat Modares University.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots for wash-free imaging of nucleolus orientation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhang, Z. Wang, H. Wang, W. Dong, Y. Liu, Q. Hu, S. Shuang, C. Dong, and X. Gong, Nitrogen-doped carbon dots for wash-free imaging of nucleolus orientation, Microchimica Acta, 188, 2021, 183.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Deep-red fluorescent gold nanoclusters for nucleoli staining: real-time monitoring of the nucleolar dynamics in reverse transformation of malignant cells
